summary: During my college days, I focused heavily on Latin, which was considered essential for acquiring culture at the time. I worked very hard to master Latin as a live, spoken language, practicing up to twelve hours a day while studying abroad in Europe. I believed in teaching Latin conversationally, encouraging students to speak it rather than just read it. Over the years, I observed the gradual decline of Latin as modern languages gained prominence, leading to the merging of Latin and Greek departments.

Greek was part of my classical studies alongside Latin during college. Like Latin, it was highly valued in the academic culture of the time. Over my career, I saw the status of Greek decline as modern languages became more prominent, which eventually led to the merging of the Greek and Latin departments. My work involved promoting the importance of these classical languages even as their popularity waned.

entity: Philological Association of the Pacific Coast (Organization)
summary: I was one of the instigators in founding the Philological Association of the Pacific Coast, which became a significant organization in academic life. It played an important role in bringing together scholars interested in language and philology on the West Coast. My involvement reflected my commitment to advancing the study of languages and fostering scholarly community in the region.

I played a vital role in the Berkeley Music Association by providing spirit and fundraising power to bring eastern concert artists to California year after year. This involvement allowed me to contribute to the cultural life of Berkeley beyond academia. It was rewarding to help enrich the community’s musical experiences through these efforts.

I helped transform the tiny storefront Berkeley Public Library into a grand structure financed by Carnegie funds. This project was designed by John Galen Howard, and it marked a significant improvement in public access to knowledge and resources. My contribution to the library reflected my commitment to education and community development in Berkeley.

The Berkeley Public Library’s transformation into a grand Carnegie library was a project I was deeply involved in. The funding from Carnegie allowed us to build a much larger and more impressive facility, designed by John Galen Howard. This library became a cornerstone for public education and learning in Berkeley, and I took pride in helping make it happen.

John Galen Howard was the architect who designed the grand Berkeley Public Library building that replaced the tiny storefront. I worked closely with the project that utilized Carnegie funds to realize this vision. His design helped create a landmark institution that greatly enhanced public access to knowledge in Berkeley.

During World War I, I handled the complex liaison agency between Washington, D.C. and the University of California military bureau. This role was critical in coordinating military-related activities and communications during the war. It was a challenging but important contribution to the war effort from the academic side.

My relationship with the University of California was extensive; I created and ran the University Extension for over fifteen years. This program developed curricula serving a diverse range of people including housewives, businessmen, immigrants, laborers, the unemployed, and prisoners. Additionally, during World War I, I coordinated between the university’s military bureau and Washington, D.C., showing my deep involvement in university affairs.

I had a close and enduring friendship with John Dewey that lasted sixty years. Our relationship was part of my rich social and intellectual life in Berkeley, where I also knew other notable figures like John Muir and William Keith. Dewey’s ideas and companionship influenced me greatly throughout my career and personal life.

John Muir was among the notable friends I had during my time in Berkeley, alongside John Dewey and William Keith. Our interactions enriched my appreciation for nature and conservation, complementing my academic and cultural pursuits. Muir’s presence in my social circle was a meaningful part of my life.

William Keith was one of my close friends in Berkeley, sharing the social and cultural circles that included John Dewey and John Muir. His artistic perspective added depth to my experiences outside academia. Our friendship was part of the vibrant intellectual and cultural community I enjoyed.

I lived in a tall white apartment building in San Francisco that offered a superb view of the Golden Gate. My study there was cluttered with maps, travel posters, books, diaries, and a grandfather clock, reflecting my lifelong interests. San Francisco was not just my home but a place that inspired my intellectual and personal pursuits.

From my apartment in San Francisco, I had a superb view of the Golden Gate, which was a daily source of inspiration and beauty. The sight of the Golden Gate connected me to the natural world even while living in the city. It was part of the backdrop to my life’s work and personal routines.
